steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage defines autofiction as patterning the protagonist's life after the author's own (typically naming the protagonist after themselves), using first-person point of view and a confessional narrative voice. It calls On Earth We're Briefly Gorgeous a “representative work” that “erases the distinction between authors and the characters they create.” The correct choice must show both the autobiographical life-patterning and the confessional voice.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘The novel portrays the upbringing of the protagonist, a young writer, in a working-class Vietnamese American family that is virtually identical to Vuong's family, and the protagonist narrates the novel in a tone that many reviewers describe as surprisingly frank.’: The protagonist's life mirrors Vuong's (autobiographical patterning ✓), and the “surprisingly frank” tone matches the confessional voice the passage describes (✓). This supports the claim that Vuong's novel is representative autofiction. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘Although the narrative of *On Earth We're Briefly Gorgeous* proceeds in a largely chronological fashion, as narratives do in most works of fiction, the form of Vuong's narrative is unique: the novel consists of a single long letter that the protagonist, a soft-spoken young man, has written to his mother.’: Describes a formal structural element (letter format) and a “soft-spoken” protagonist — this does not establish autobiographical life-patterning or a confessional voice, so it doesn't support the autofiction claim. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘*On Earth We're Briefly Gorgeous* makes use of imagery in much the same way that contemporary poetry does, exploiting certain images—butterflies for example—for their emotional or conceptual associations; in this way, Vuong blurs the distinction between poetry and fiction.’: Describes poetic imagery technique, which is irrelevant to the defining features of autofiction described in the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘The novel's protagonist bears an obvious resemblance to Vuong, and in interviews Vuong speaks of the protagonist as a sort of alter ego; the protagonist refers to himself as "Little Dog" throughout the novel and never divulges his actual name.’: The passage says autofiction authors typically name their protagonists after themselves. A protagonist who never reveals his real name actively avoids this convention, which contradicts rather than supports the autofiction description. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
